summary: MAUI SKGLView on iOS fails with 'Failed to bind EAGLDrawable' and 'Failed to make complete framebuffer object' on .NET 10, resulting in a white canvas. The iOS SKGLViewHandler uses the deprecated OpenGL ES (GLKView/EAGLContext) backend, which appears increasingly broken on newer iOS SDKs shipped with .NET 10.

stepsToReproduce: Create a MAUI app using SKGLView on iOS with .NET 10
Observe white canvas and EAGL error messages in console
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: .NET 10, iOS (version unspecified), SkiaSharp 3.116.0, MAUI, Visual Studio macOS

repoLinks.description: Mapsui MapControl using SKGLView with OnGLPaintSurface and OnPaintSurface

bugSignals.errorMessage: Failed to bind EAGLDrawable to GL_RENDERBUFFER 1; Failed to make complete multisample framebuffer object 8cd6; Failed to make complete framebuffer object 8cd6

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The iOS SKGLViewHandler still uses the deprecated OpenGL ES (GLKView/EAGLContext) path. This code has not changed and the underlying Apple API deprecation is worsening with newer iOS SDKs.

regression.reason: The regression is likely caused by .NET 10 shipping with a newer iOS SDK that further degrades OpenGL ES support, not by a SkiaSharp code change. The SKGLView OpenGL ES code path is unchanged. The 2.88.9 'worked' version was likely tested on an older .NET/iOS SDK combination.

analysis.summary: The MAUI iOS SKGLViewHandler uses SKGLView which is based on GLKView with EAGLContext (OpenGL ES 2). Apple deprecated OpenGL ES since iOS 12.0. The error messages indicate the EAGL context cannot bind the drawable to a renderbuffer, causing framebuffer creation to fail. The MacCatalyst handler already uses SKMetalView (Metal) instead, confirming the migration path exists but was not applied to iOS.
analysis.rationale: This is a real rendering failure on iOS .NET 10 caused by the deprecated OpenGL ES backend. The error is in Apple's EAGL layer, not in SkiaSharp's code per se, but SkiaSharp's iOS handler still relies on this deprecated pathway. The MacCatalyst handler already migrated to Metal. Classified as type/bug because the SKGLView API is supposed to provide GPU-accelerated rendering on iOS but fails completely on .NET 10.

keySignals.text: Failed to bind EAGLDrawable: <CAEAGLLayer> to GL_RENDERBUFFER 1
keySignals.interpretation: The OpenGL ES context cannot bind the view's CAEAGLLayer to a renderbuffer — this is the EAGL initialization failing, not a SkiaSharp rendering bug.

keySignals.text: Failed to make complete multisample framebuffer object 8cd6
keySignals.interpretation: GL error 0x8CD6 (GL_FRAMEBUFFER_INCOMPLETE_ATTACHMENT) — the renderbuffer attachment is invalid because the EAGLDrawable binding failed. The 4x multisample configuration compounds the issue.
Works on .NET 9 but not .NET 10, suggesting the newer iOS SDK shipped with .NET 10 has further restricted OpenGL ES support.
SkiaSharp itself marks this API as obsolete on iOS 12+ and recommends Metal, confirming this is a known deprecation path.

codeInvestigation.finding: iOS SKGLView extends GLKView and creates an EAGLContext with OpenGLES2 (line 94). It also sets DrawableMultisample to Sample4x (line 98), which may exacerbate the framebuffer creation failure. The file is guarded with #if !__MACCATALYST__ (line 1), confirming Mac Catalyst already uses a different path.
The MAUI iOS handler wraps SKGLView (the OpenGL ES-based view). CreatePlatformView returns a MauiSKGLView which inherits from SKGLView (GLKView/EAGL). This is the code path that fails on .NET 10.
The MacCatalyst handler already uses SKMetalView instead of SKGLView. This proves the Metal migration path works and has been applied to one Apple platform but not iOS.
SKMetalView uses MTKView with Metal backend. It handles device creation, command queue, and proper framebuffer setup. This is the replacement for SKGLView on Apple platforms.

workarounds: Use SKCanvasView instead of SKGLView for CPU-based rendering (avoids OpenGL ES entirely)
The iOS SKGLViewHandler could potentially be modified to use SKMetalView like the MacCatalyst handler does
nextQuestions: Which specific iOS version and device is this occurring on?
Does the issue also occur on .NET 10 with the iOS simulator vs a physical device?
Would migrating the iOS SKGLViewHandler to use SKMetalView (like MacCatalyst) be a feasible fix?

resolution.hypothesis: The .NET 10 iOS SDK further degrades or restricts OpenGL ES support, causing EAGLDrawable binding to fail. The iOS MAUI handler still uses the deprecated GLKView/EAGLContext path while MacCatalyst already migrated to Metal.

proposals.title: Use SKCanvasView instead of SKGLView
proposals.description: Replace SKGLView with SKCanvasView in the Mapsui MapControl for iOS. This uses CPU rendering instead of GPU via OpenGL ES, avoiding the deprecated EAGL path entirely. Performance may be lower for complex rendering but eliminates the framebuffer failure.
proposals.codeSnippet:
// In MapControl.cs, use SKCanvasView instead of SKGLView for iOS:
// Replace _glView with _canvasView and handle OnPaintSurface instead of OnGLPaintSurface

proposals.title: Migrate iOS SKGLViewHandler to Metal
proposals.description: Modify SKGLViewHandler.iOS.cs to use SKMetalView instead of SKGLView, following the pattern already established in SKGLViewHandler.MacCatalyst.cs. This would provide GPU-accelerated rendering via Metal instead of the deprecated OpenGL ES.

resolution.recommendedReason: Immediate workaround the reporter can apply in their Mapsui MapControl. The proper fix (Metal migration) requires changes in SkiaSharp itself.

actionability.reason: Real rendering failure caused by deprecated OpenGL ES on iOS .NET 10. Needs investigation into whether migrating the iOS handler to Metal (like MacCatalyst) is feasible and whether this affects all .NET 10 iOS builds.

actions.description: Labels are already correct: type/bug, os/iOS, area/SkiaSharp.Views.Maui, tenet/reliability. Add backend/OpenGL.

actions.comment:
Thanks for reporting this. The error messages point to a failure in Apple's OpenGL ES layer — the `EAGLContext` can't bind the `CAEAGLLayer` to a renderbuffer, which causes the framebuffer to be incomplete and results in the white canvas.

The MAUI `SKGLView` on iOS still uses GLKit with OpenGL ES 2, which Apple deprecated in iOS 12.0. The Mac Catalyst handler already migrated to Metal (`SKMetalView`), but the iOS handler has not. It's likely that .NET 10's newer iOS SDK has further degraded OpenGL ES support.

Here's a workaround you can use while we investigate: switch from `SKGLView` to `SKCanvasView` in your Mapsui MapControl for iOS. This uses CPU-based rendering instead of OpenGL ES and avoids the deprecated EAGL path entirely. Performance may differ for complex map rendering, but it should eliminate the framebuffer failure.

The proper fix would be to migrate the iOS `SKGLViewHandler` to use `SKMetalView` (Metal) instead of `SKGLView` (OpenGL ES), matching what Mac Catalyst already does. We'll investigate that path.

Could you confirm which iOS version and device you're seeing this on, and whether it also occurs in the iOS Simulator?

actions.description: Link to SKGLView MAUI implementation PR

missingInfo: Specific iOS version and device model
Whether the issue occurs on iOS simulator vs physical device
Whether SKCanvasView works as a workaround
